issue_title: Provide a way to filter out (or easily spot) messages triggered by jQuery UI or jQuery Mobile
issue_body:
Use case: I am using jQuery + jQueryUI + my own code depending on both, and I want to migrate to jQuery 3.

So, I'll use the jQuery Migrate plugin and look at the messages it generates pointing out migration issues.

Result: I am flooded by tons of warnings caused by jQuery UI using deprecated features. JqueryUI won't get rid of them and for a good reason, because they have to maintain bc with older jQuery versions.

Right now, the only way to distinguish between warning issued by jQuery Migrate that are triggered by my code, and hence need my attention, and those triggered by jQuery UI, which I have to ignore, is to inspect each trace one by one. That is painful and unviable.

Since there are official jQuery products out there (jQueryUI and probably jQueryMobile) that are and will keep using deprecated jQuery features, jQuery Migrate should provide a way to easily filter out or "spot" messages triggered by those product. It should recognize when a warning is caused by jQuery UI or jQuery Mobile, and there should be an option (probably enabled by default) to filter out these messages completely. Or, as a second-best alternative (actually, this would be desirable regardless of the former), the name of the file that has triggered the message should be included in the message itself instead of being buried in the trace. So, just by looking at the list of migration warning without inspecting them, you could more easily spot the ones that need attention.

issue_title: make api.user.has_permission work with permission ids too
issue_body:
currently `api.user.has_permission('cmf.ModifyPortalContent')` returns `True` if logged in as `Manager`  and `False` for users having the `Modify portal content` permission.

problem: the method can only handle permission titles and silently fails when called with permission ids (https://github.com/zopefoundation/Products.CMFCore/blob/2.3.0/Products/CMFCore/permissions.zcml#L30)

i previously used the following code in my project:

```python
from AccessControl.security import checkPermission

def allowed(context):
    return checkPermission('cmf.ModifyPortalContent', context)
```

when i started to replace this checks with `plone.api.user.has_permission` i did not notice there is a problem first because i was logged in as manager user.
now our customer found out that ui elements where missing for content editors because the updated permission checks did not work properly.


this is why i'd suggest to make plone.api.user.has_permission to work with permission titles as well as ids
if that is not possible it would also help when `plone.api.user.has_permission('invalid permission string', context)` raises an exception if the permission is not valid (i can file another ticket for this if you agree with that)

some pseudo-test-code:

```python
setRoles(['Contributor'])
assertEqual(
   api.user.has_permission('cmf.ModifyPortalContent'),
   api.user.has_permission('Modify portal content'),
assertRaises(
  api.user.has_permission,
  'non existent permission',
  PermissionNotFoundError
) 
```

`plone.api.user.has_permission('invalid permission string', context)` should raise an exception if the permission id is not valid (i can file another ticket if you agree with that)

issue_title: Feature request: make DocType obj.get("unkown_id") return None
issue_body:
Currently, if we try to get a document with an unknown id, a NotFoundError (TransportError) is raised.

This means that in many cases, a simple get(id) should be handled with a ```try:... except:...```:
 
```
>>> tu=TestUser.get("test")
GET /testing/test_users/test [status:404 request:0.002s]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl-0.0.4.dev0-py3.3.egg/elasticsearch_dsl/document.py", line 115, in get
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch/client/utils.py", line 68, in _wrapped
    return func(*args, params=params, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch/client/__init__.py", line 322, in get
    params=params)
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch/transport.py", line 301, in perform_request
    status, headers, data = connection.perform_request(method, url, params, body, ignore=ignore, timeout=timeout)
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch/connection/http_urllib3.py", line 82, in perform_request
    self._raise_error(response.status, raw_data)
  File "/Users/Nicolas/myproject/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch/connection/base.py", line 102, in _raise_error
    raise HTTP_EXCEPTIONS.get(status_code, TransportError)(status_code, error_message, additional_info)
elasticsearch.exceptions.NotFoundError: TransportError(404, '{"_index":"testing","_type":"test_users","_id":"test","found":false}')
>>> 
>>> try:
...     tu = TestUser.get("test")
... except TransportError:
...     tu = None
... 
GET /testing/test_users/test [status:404 request:0.002s]
>>> tu
>>> 
```

I was wondering if it might be better to have it return None instead, in which case the above ```try:... except:...```
 would become:

```
>>> tu = TestUser.get("test")
>>> tu is None
True
```

What are your thoughts on this suggestion?

issue_title: ValidationError override multiple errors on the same field
issue_body:
Hi!

When you have a validation error with multiple errors on the same field, only the last message is kept.

Eg. On a subscription error you can have a xml like that:
```xml

<errors>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.address1" symbol="empty">can't be empty</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.city" symbol="empty">can't be empty</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.country" symbol="empty">can't be empty</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.first_name" symbol="blank">can't be blank</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.last_name" symbol="blank">can't be blank</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.number" symbol="required">is required</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.billing_info.zip" symbol="empty">can't be empty</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.account_code" symbol="blank">can't be blank</error>
  <error field="subscription.account.account_code" symbol="invalid">is invalid</error>
  <error field="subscription.plan_code" symbol="invalid">is invalid</error>
  <error field="subscription.unit_amount_in_cents" symbol="not_a_number">is not a number</error>
</errors>
```
And the python representation of the error is:
```python

In [98]: try:
    ...:     recurly.Subscription(account=recurly.Account(billing_info=recurly.BillingInfo())).save()
    ...: except recurly.ValidationError as err:
    ...:     pass
    ...:     

In [99]: print(err)
not_a_number: subscription.unit_amount_in_cents is not a number; invalid: subscription.plan_code is invalid; required: subscription.account.billing_info.number is required; empty: subscription.account.billing_info.address1 can't be empty; blank: subscription.account.billing_info.last_name can't be blank; blank: subscription.account.billing_info.first_name can't be blank; empty: subscription.account.billing_info.country can't be empty; empty: subscription.account.billing_info.zip can't be empty; empty: subscription.account.billing_info.city can't be empty; invalid: subscription.account.account_code is invalid

In [100]: err.errors
Out[100]: 
{'subscription.account.account_code': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b0ece8c90>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.address1': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b1c161cd0>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.city': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b1c161590>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.country': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b1c161850>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.first_name': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b1c161390>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.last_name': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b1c161190>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.number': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b0ece8290>,
 'subscription.account.billing_info.zip': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b0ece8dd0>,
 'subscription.plan_code': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b0ece8810>,
 'subscription.unit_amount_in_cents': <recurly.errors.Suberror at 0x7f0b0ece8fd0>}

```
We see that we only have one ```subscription.account.account_code``` instead of two

Could we have a list of suberrors as a value of the dict instead of just one suberror ?

The code is located here: https://github.com/recurly/recurly-client-python/blob/master/recurly/errors.py#L238
```python
suberrors = dict()
for err in self.response_doc.findall('error'):
    field = err.attrib['field']
    symbol = err.attrib['symbol']
    message = err.text

    suberrors[field] = self.Suberror(field, symbol, message)

self.__dict__['errors'] = suberrors
```
Maybe use a defaultdict(list) instead of a dict ?

issue_title: Clarification needed: multiget - calling queue.task_done without ever calling queue.join()
issue_body:
As seen in the [documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/queue.html#Queue.Queue.task_done), task_done is only used/cared for when you might be doing a Queue.join() call (looking at the python source code confirms this also). Which is never seen within multi.py, however I do see multiple instances of the task_done call; [example](https://github.com/basho/riak-python-client/blob/f13f0df8a566f0002210cb76bb942e9db8c25ea4/riak/client/multi.py#L222).

Is there some other undocumented side-affect of calling task_done in each of these places? Or is this likely an artefact of some older feature/ port from another language?

I realise that calling task_done doesn't cause anything to break, and keeps the queue in a state with a "more correct" view of what tasks it has processed, but i'm wondering if it _is_ used for something unexpected.

issue_title: Cannot run LOAD CSV query across multiple sessions in the same application.
issue_body:
Using Neo4j 3.0.7.

I'm having trouble with LOAD CSV queries and multiple session in the same application. It looks like if you run a LOAD CSV query through a driver session, the connection locks the entire Neo4j import folder, not just the files you are loading. This becomes problematic when I want to bulk load data with multiple requests in my Python application. I've tried closing the driver's pool of sessions manually, but to no avail. My process will still hang on the second session that attempts to load CSV files.

As an aside, I believe this problem occurs primarily because of the connection not completely being closed on deletion or garbage collection, but on a complete break of the application.

Is there an "easy fix" that I can implement to resolve this issue without upgrading the Neo4j server?

issue_title: Can exception handling be extensible?

In the current design I don't see a nice way to do this though so was hoping someone else might have some thoughts on how this could be implemented.....

              << "  " << *transform[4]  << " \t" << *transform[5]  << " \t" << *transform[6]  << " \t" << *transform[7]  << std::endl

issue_title: EC2 import volume from VMDK image in S3
issue_body:
Hello,

We have a problem when we upload vmdk image to S3, and import that image from S3 to EC2. We need instructions, after reading boto3 documents, and tried many times but still have some troubles… Thank you so much!!
 
We are using:
--------------------------------
Debian Wheezy
Python 2.7
Boto3 installed in system

Our purpose:
--------------------------------
We want to upload one 2G vmdk image to S3, and import volume to EC2 by using that vmdk image, just like aws CLI command tool “ec2-import-volume”.

Our attempts and problem:
--------------------------------
s3_client = boto3.client('s3')
s3_client.create_bucket(Bucket='xxxx')
s3_client.upload_file('/local/path/image.vmdk', 'xxxx', 'image')                 # We also want to use “boto3.s3.transfer.S3Transfer” to do this work, much better actually

After the image uploaded to s3, we tried to use boto3 client ec2's "import_volume" function:
ec2_client = boto3.client('ec2')
ec2_client.import_volume(AvailabilityZone="us-east-1e", Image={'Format': 'VMDK', 'Bytes': 2333108736, 'ImportManifestUrl':'https://xxxx.s3.amazonaws.com/image'}, Description="Boto3Test", Volume={'Size': 20})
 
From the document of boto3, the "ImportManifestUrl" above should be “presigned manifest URL”:
 
We create the presigned URL for that image: 
s3_client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={'Bucket':'xxxx', 'Key': 'image'})
 
We found that we don’t have a manifest xml file for the uploaded vmdk image.
 
Previously the “ec2-import-volume” will automatically upload the image to S3 in multi-parts, and also creates the manifest xml file, however, seems the boto3 S3 client “upload_file” function won’t do that for us.

Questions:
-------------------------------
1.       Is there anything wrong in my steps above? (What is the correct way to do the same work with “ec2-import-volume”?)
2.       Is there a way to create the manifest xml file for the image in boto3? Or, I need to create it manually?
3.       If I have to create the manifest file manually, can you give us some detailed instructions to create it?
 
Again, thank you very much! We really appreciate your help and time!
 
Regards,

Derek

issue_title: Batch documentation
issue_body:
The API documentation for the [AWS Batch submit-job action](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/batch/latest/APIReference/API_SubmitJob.html#Batch-SubmitJob-request-dependsOn) describes the option `dependsOn` as:
> A list of job IDs on which this job depends. A job can depend upon a maximum of 100 jobs.

The Boto3 documentation for the same [submit-job action](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/batch.html#Batch.Client.submit_job) describes the option as:
> A list of job names or IDs on which this job depends. A job can depend upon a maximum of 100 jobs.

Now I am wondering if it is really possible to provide a list of job names? Does the API really support that or are only IDs possible?

issue_title: normalize ISBNs to ISBN13 without dashes
issue_body: On legacy, various ISBN formats are used: ISBN 10 and ISBN 13, without separator or separated by spaces or dashes. They should all be normalized to ISBN 13 without separator, of the form "978123456789X". Care has to be taken that some ISBNs on legacy are invalid: those should be migrated as is (and logged if possible).

issue_title: Lost the ability of processing unicode choices
issue_body:
Regarding of process.extract, when we call scorer, which is fuzz.WRatio actually, the exact locations are [process.py#L104](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/process.py#L104) and [process.py#L110](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/process.py#L110), we leave the third argument force_ascii to default value which is True according to [fuzz.py#L236](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L236) thus we can not process Unicode choices.

Solution:
1. provide one new parameter for process.extract at [process.py#L33](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/process.py#L33), means change
    `def extract(query, choices, processor=None, scorer=None, limit=5):`
to
    `def extract(query, choices, processor=None, scorer=None, limit=5, force_ascii=True):`
2. take the argument for fuzz.WRatio, means change [process.py#L104](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/process.py#L104) and [process.py#L110](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/process.py#L110) from
     `score = scorer(query, processed)`
to
     `score = scorer(query, processed, force_ascii)`

### Issue description

issue_title: Ability to load PIPENV_* environs from .env file
issue_body:
I understand the existence of #902, but the it seems the OP was looking for something different.

Sometimes I want Pipenv to handle one particular project differently than others. The most common problem is I don’t usually set `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT`, but for some projects I want to do. This is however not possible, because they are loaded for `shell` and `run`, not `install` etc., and even if they are, `.env` is loaded during runtime, therefore doesn’t affect values in `environments.py`.

`direnv` and similar tools like `autoenv` are not an option for me because they are all shell-depedent, and I operate in the command prompt on Windows.

My current workaround is to use [a custom Dotenv loader](https://github.com/uranusjr/python-dotenv-run), and a Batch script like this:

```batch
REM pipenv.bat
REM Shim to wrap Pipenv invocation in a loaded environment.
REM Put this in the project root so it’s picked up when you call "pipenv".

if exist ".env" (
  dotenv-run pipenv %*
) else (
    pipenv %*
)
```

which sort of works, but is suboptimal. I wish Pipenv could handle this more cleanly since it already loads .env files anyway.

##### Steps to replicate

1. Create a project
2. Run `pipenv install` with `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` set
3. Unset `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT`, create `.env` with `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT=1`

##### Expected result

```batch
> pipenv install
Loading .env environment variables…
Installing dependencies from Pipfile.lock
```

Pipenv should go on reusing `.venv` and perform operations.

##### Actual result

```batch
> pipenv install
Creating a virtualenv for this project…
```

Pipenv goes on to create a virtualenv in the default location, not respecting values in `.env`.

issue_title: [question] What is the recommended best practice for Pipenv and apps on Pypi?
issue_body:
Throughout my learning about Pipenv, I have found various viewpoints about how one should handle applications that are supposed to be on Pypi (such as CLI apps). My initial thoughts are that one should get their dependencies from their Pipfile to their setup.py (because I want pip installs that are deterministic), but unfortunately, this is met with a ["Do not do this."](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/209#issuecomment-332553113). I also understand why; The setup.py should only depend on setuptools and wheel; A dependency on Pipenv would not work.

That same thread later has [a message from Kenneth Reitz which claims](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/209#issuecomment-337409290):

> If I've explained this well enough, you can see that its just supposed to be the other way around.
You put your dependencies in install_requires, you put you package in the Pipfile, and then you get and environment, with a Pipfile.lock for reproducibility (as it will resolve and respect your package dependencies).

Ok..., so I should run a `pipenv install -e .`, and still place dependencies in my `install_requires`. I get reproducible builds with my `Pipfile.lock`, but..., what about pip? **How do I get deterministic pip installs for a package without necessarily having to pin package versions AND not dependending on pipenv?**

Looking at the repository for pipenv itself, I can't seem to find anything useful, as this package specifies its dependencies in `setup.py` (and nothing in `packages` of the Pipfile, not even an install of the current package through `-e .`). It's also evident that pipenv hasn't pinned package versions in your setup.py either, so the problems pipenv can solve aren't being solved for dependencies in this project.

Another idea: Given the new `pyproject.toml`, can I specify `pipenv` in the build requirements, and then import it into my project to add its dependencies to the `setup.py`?

Yes, I've read things like https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/, but that doesn't necessarily help me, because it focuses on getting requirements.txt from setup.py, not the other way around, which would get me what I want.

Essentially, I want a way for `pip install my_cli_package` to get dependencies from the `Pipfile.lock` for that current package somehow. It's possible that my current way of thinking is completely wrong, and if it is, I would appreciate some clarification as to what I really _should_ be aiming for (which is why I'm looking for the current best practice).

Thanks!

From the (excellent) documentation, setup.py is really intended for Python libraries, packages that are imported by other packages. Obviously if I am building a Python library and uploading it to PyPI, I must create a `setup.py`. (In addition to my existing `pipfile` as required for `pipenv`.)

But what if I am building an application and uploading it to PyPI? For example, so that a user can `pip install --user myapp` and then launch `myapp`. Do I still need `setup.py` in this case, or will the `pip install` succeed with just a `pipfile`?

issue_title: Allow alternative requirements
issue_body:
I recently hit an issue when requiring a module for MIME magic detection. There are the file-magic and the magic distribution. Because the developers are idiots, both distributions contain the magic package, with entirely different APIs.

While I could detect this in my code and generate matching wrapper code, I wanted to also declare a requirement in setup.py that can be satisfied by botI came up with the following snippet: https://gist.github.com/Natureshadow/92f30ce650682a638b0bc75e6d54e8b4

The idea is to check all provided alternatives, and if one is installed, return that, and if not, return the first, which has highest precedence (the same logic used by APT).

You can just add a call to `requires_alternative("file-magic", "magic")` to the `install_requires` list.

Feel free to incorporate this into setuptools; if the implementation is not on par with your code, please take this as a feature request.

issue_title: EPERM when calling os.chflags() on a file with SF_RESTRICTED set
issue_body:
On macOS 10.13, some files in `/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/lib-dynload` (which `virtualenv.py` may attempt to copy) have the `SF_RESTRICTED` stat file set. When `shutil.copystat()` is eventually called, it will attempt to carry over `SF_RESTRICTED` as part of calling `os.chflags()`. This will raise an `OSError` with `EPERM` as the error code because normal users can't set the `SF_RESTRICTED` stat flag.

`SF_RESTRICTED` is not defined in CPython - even on the `master` branch as of this writing. Its value is `0x80000` (`2^19`). The Python standard library is blissfully unaware of this flag's existence.

One potential workaround for this is to monkeypatch `os.chflags()` in `virtualenv.py` to drop `SF_RESTRICTED` from the flags. e.g. `flags ^= 0x80000`. This looks to be the approach we're going to take at Mozilla (https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1459071). (Having `SF_RESTRICTED` set shouldn't matter for the virtualenv's copied files.)

issue_title: bin/upgrade script authentication handshake
issue_body:
When using the `bin/upgrade` command authentication is required for some commands (e.g. `list` and `install`).
Currently the authentication information can be provided by using `--auth` or by setting the environment variable.
This results in having an administrator password in the bash history or somewhere on the machine, which is not very nice. It also requires to have an administrator user with known password (we want to get rid of this for privacy reasons).

The script should be able to make a handshake with a running Plone instance and automatically authenticate (e.g. as app or site owner).
I think verifying that the client script (`bin/upgrade`) can read private files from the filesystem where the server runs we have enough security (meaning: the user has access to the server and could make a `bin/instance makeuser` anyway and would have full access).
Therefore we could do this:
- `bin/upgrade` requests an authentication session from the Zope instance
- The Zope instance writes a private file to the filesystem, containing a hash
- `bin/upgrade` reads the file and sends the hash in subsequent requests
- The Zope instance automatically authenticates as Manager user
- The file is removed when the session is ended.

issue_title: Consider returning UTC datetimes
issue_body:
I have been bitten by the assumption that UTCDateTime always has a representation of the DateTime in UTC. It actually hasn't. While everything is sent to the database as UTC it isnt returned as UTC when the database does a transformation. This can lead to unexpected results, ie getting a datetime back with a different timezone offset than it was originally stored in.

In my case I was storing UTC datetimes, but the DB was configured 'Europe/Amsterdam' and thus was returning datetimes in 'Europe/Amsterdam'.

issue_title: Mock Subprocess Context Manager
issue_body:
With a little experimentation I was able to mock a subprocess being used as a context manager. I'd like to open a PR and add an example to the docs but thought I'd put up here first to see if there might be a cleaner way to achieve this?

```python
def my_func():
```

```python

def fake_context_exit(mock_instance, *args, **kw):

class TestMyFunc(TestCase):
```

issue_title: Performance issue with centroid
issue_body:
I have been trying to use  `photutils.detection.find_peaks` function with the `subpixel=True` option and ran into a performance issue which prevent me to use it in a Monte Carlo simulation. I tracked this down to the `photutils.centroid.fit_2dgaussian` which use `astropy.modeling` to fit a custom 2d gaussian + constant model at each of the peaks.

Looking at `photutils.centroid.GaussianConst2D` class I wondered if this custom made compound was the culprit, so I ran a little benchmark to compare it to other definition of compound models and utilmately to `scipy.optimize.curve_fit`.  [Gist here](https://gist.github.com/abeelen/f3bf445cb0628dc4fe7a4fc3a89be28c) 

It turns out that for small data set, which `find_peaks` is doing, the overhead is huge :
* 13x compared to `curve_fit`,
* or even 3x compared to a simple  `astropy.modeling.custom_model` decorator

Things are worse when adding a little bit of noise.

I could work out a PR to write a proper model for `GaussianConst2D` but i would still be (much, 6x) slower than a simple curve_fit. What would be the best way to proceed here ?

issue_title: High memory usage during DAISY computation for large images
issue_body:
First of all I would like to thank all contributors for this excellent package and the continuous extension of features, I have been using scikit-image in my research for several years (and citing the webpage/paper).

Recently I used the HOG and DAISY descriptors for image classification and noticed that DAISY computation requires a lot of memory when working with large images (6 - 9 MPixel) due to its (though elegant) use of temporary arrays for intermediate results and vectorized computation.

I attached the output of [memory_profiler](https://github.com/fabianp/memory_profiler) for an image with 9Mpixels, which used **13.5 GiB** during computation. As I run out of memory when using `orientations` > 18, I am looking for workarounds. These do not necessarily have to involve changes to the codebase, but maybe a reduced memory footprint would benefit others as well.

Simple optimizations:
* delete intermediate results once they are no longer used (I had good results with explicitly calling `gc.collect()` in my batch scripts with many large images)
* split the computation for large images and merge resulting descriptors

What do you suggest?
(ping @andersbll as he authored the original PR)

However, when running on an image with more regions, the method returns the correct count of the number of regions, but the labeled array it returns contains no labeled region numbered higher than 255.

However, the output array contains 254 clusters labeled 1 through 254, respectively.  All other clusters (266345 - 254 of them) are numbered '255'.  No errors of any kind are thrown.  I would call this a bug.

issue_title: update numpy to latest 1.9
We do nothing when a Python region reassigns an output
issue_body:
This is in response to the first issue in: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/3078 

This issue has come up before: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/commit/ba3d023f638b78d5311d69777e18d8a76df9513e#diff-9eaab542ebc9927987ca92e4cf244759R47

We should stop failing silently when an output is reassigned. It's easy to accidentally do this in Python.

I prototyped a fix to support this usage: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/918

But it's gross. There should be one right way.

issue_title: Fix encoder implementation to standards
issue_body:
the encoders implementation in `src/nupic/encoders/` is quite a mess, I guess the PR process was quite relaxed :tongue: 

- [ ] `ScalarSensor` (region) should not be in `nupic/encoders/` when `nupic/regions/` exists. 
- [ ] Encoders write output to `Real32[]` - it's a waste and has to be converted everywhere, see #948 
- [ ] missing use of more restrictive/descriptive `UInt` where suitable
- [ ] does not implement `encode()` which is API in python nupic

issue_title: Incompatibilities with recent CLDR releases
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.i18n/+bug/1073940, Murithi reported:

> The versions of LDML files in locales/data are pretty old (released in 2004).  Getting any new version from unicode.org: http://unicode.org/repos/cldr/trunk/common/main/ reveals a number of issues:
> 
> - Parsing the generation in this format:

```xml
<generation date="$Date: 2012-10-11 23:11:48 +0300 (Thu, 11 Oct 2012) $"/>
```

> fails:

```python
  File ".../eggs/zope.i18n-3.7.4-py2.7.egg/zope/i18n/locales/xmlfactory.py", line 79, in _extractVersion
    generationDate = date(int(year), int(month), int(day))
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '$Date: 2012'
```

> - There is an assumption that a date format e.g.:

```xml
      <dateFormat>
       <pattern>MMMM d, y</pattern>
      </dateFormat>
```

> implies length for all the date parts - it does not and will fail when formatting dates.

```python
  File ".../eggs/zope.i18n-3.7.4-py2.7.egg/zope/i18n/format.py", line 192, in format
    text += info.get(elem, elem)
TypeError: coercing to Unicode: need string or buffer, tuple found
<type 'exceptions.TypeError'>:
coercing to Unicode: need string or buffer, tuple found
> /home/undesa/bungeni_apps/bungeni/eggs/zope.i18n-3.7.4-py2.7.egg/zope/i18n/format.py(192)format()
-> text += info.get(elem, elem)
```

> This likely never bombed before since a lookup of the info dictionary always returned a string.

> - `getFormatter (zope/i18n/locales/__init__.py LINE 495)`
> There are a number of issues with lookup here:

```python
        formats = getattr(cal, category+'Formats')
        if length is None:
            length = getattr(
                cal,
                'default'+category[0].upper()+category[1:]+'Format',
                formats.keys()[0])

        # 'datetime' is always a bit special; we often do not have a length
        # specification, but we need it for looking up the date and time
        # formatters
        if category == 'dateTime':
            formatLength = formats.get(length, formats[None])
```

> For instance: 

```python
length = getattr(cal, 'default'+category[0].upper()+category[1:]+'Format', formats.keys()[0])
```

> Why should this fall back to looking up the format key from the calendar instance?
> Also, `formats[None]` raises an AttributeError

issue_title: ZCML principal directive should define `id` to be unicode on Python 2
issue_body:
See zopefoundation/zope.security#4.

In short, the `IPrincipal` interface defines `id` to be a `TextLine`, aka `unicode` on Python 2. But the `IDefinePrincipalDirective` from this package defines `id` to be a, well, `Id`, which is a `NativeStringLine`, aka `BytesLine` on Python 2, meaning that the resulting principal objects aren't valid according to the interface.

The `zope.principalregistry.tests` contains a `principals.zcml` file with these partial contents:
```xml
  <include package="zope.principalregistry" file="meta.zcml"/>

  <principal
      id="zope.p1"
      title="Sir Tim Peters"
      description="Tim Peters"
      login="tim"
      password_manager="SHA1"
      password="40bd001563085fc35165329ea1ff5c5ecbdbbeef"
      />
```

Let's load that file:
```pycon
Python 2.7.13 (default, May  5 2017, 09:16:47)
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from zope.configuration import xmlconfig
>>> import zope.principalregistry.tests
>>> xmlconfig.file('principal.zcml', zope.principalregistry.tests)
```

Now lets get the resulting principal. We'll note that his resulting `id` is bytes (`str` since this is Python 2):
```pycon
>>> import zope.principalregistry.principalregistry
>>> prin = zope.principalregistry.principalregistry.principalRegistry.getPrincipalByLogin('tim')
>>> prin.id
'zope.p1'
>>> type(prin.id)
<type 'str'>
>>> type(prin.id) is bytes
True
```

Now this object passes the validity tests in zope.interface because it doesn't understand schemas:
```pycon
>>> from zope.security.interfaces import IPrincipal
>>> from zope.interface.verify import verifyObject
>>> verifyObject(IPrincipal, prin)
True
```

But as soon as you bring schema validation in, you see that the id is incorrect:
```pycon
>>> IPrincipal['id']
<zope.schema._bootstrapfields.TextLine object at 0x1076e9210>
>>> IPrincipal['id'].validate(prin.id)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "//site-packages/zope/schema/_bootstrapfields.py", line 183, in validate
    self._validate(value)
  File "//site-packages/zope/schema/_bootstrapfields.py", line 310, in _validate
    super(MinMaxLen, self)._validate(value)
  File "//site-packages/zope/schema/_bootstrapfields.py", line 210, in _validate
    raise WrongType(value, self._type, self.__name__)
zope.schema._bootstrapinterfaces.WrongType: ('zope.p1', <type 'unicode'>, 'id')
```

issue_title: `get`-ing a resource that was cached in `RedisCache` refreshes that key's TTL
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

- instantiate a gapipy using `gapipy.cache.RedisCache` as cache backend and some default TTL, e.g. `'cache_options': { 'default_timeout': 60*60 }`
- fetch a resource so that it is inserted into Redis, e.g. `gapipy_instance.itineraries.get(1198, 946, cached=False)`
- check the TTL of that key (e.g. `redis-cli ttl '*itineraries:1198:946*'`) -- it will be the default timeout you set minus the few seconds that have passed since calling `get`
- wait a few seconds
- fetch the same resource again but tell gapipy to use its cache, e.g. `gapipy_instance.itineraries.get(1198, 946, cached=True)`
- check the TTL of that key again, the default timeout you set minus the few seconds that have passed since your most recent call to `get`

Expected behaviour:
- subsequent gets that are served from cache should not disturb the TTL of the key, in the above example, our second check of the TTL should be lower than the first check

Effect:
- a resource cached in Redis that is fetched from cache more than once-per-TTL will never fall out of the cache

issue_title: AttributeError: module 'django.conf.global_settings' has no attribute 'TEMPLATE_CONTEXT_PROCESSORS'
issue_body:
On `django 1.11.5`:

The following error is shown with runserver:

```
AttributeError: module 'django.conf.global_settings' has no attribute 'TEMPLATE_CONTEXT_PROCESSORS'
```

So I left it out as it was apparently [deprecated in django 1.8 and removed in 1.10](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41487104/attributeerror-settings-object-has-no-attribute-template-context-processors)

Now I get a `Direct state modification is not allowed` error, when using the buttons provided on admin.

issue_title: boost includes not found
issue_body:
Building libtorrent depending on boost as found here: https://github.com/brussee/SkeletonApp/tree/master/recipes
fails because basic includes are not found.

This particular include is located in /boost/compatibility/cpp_c_headers/
Including this directory manually leads to another one, and another one, etc.


issue_title: Feature request: access to all permissions
issue_body:
Don't have an option to write a PR(debugged), so I rather ask beforehand. When I use custom launcher, I put there every permission I know could be at least a little bit useful. As the launcher isn't intended for final distribution, e.g. `__init__.py` could be a nice place for a list of all available permissions.

Then `--permission ALL` would be possible as it'd only need to have a simple loop in each `build.py` that can build the launcher(or even in all builds). However, I can see ALL not available in arguments as a safety switch too for the final dist.

Is it a good idea to include ALL into p4a?  pros could be simple process of adding permissions, maybe future check if permission is typed correctly(in a list of permissions) and maybe prevent crashes this way(don't know if there is some check for this), then make updating _custom_(I don't want you to go for stable launcher with all permissions) one less frequent. This could make the launcher slowly become "ide" on android for Kivy.

Two critical cons are basically disabling permission security at all with this(app would have ALL) and a risk that some beginner would go for this craziness in final dist.

issue_title: q9 significantly faster than q6 for specific input
issue_body:
I've been in touch with Jyrki Alakuijala on twitter after I noticed compressing with q9 being faster than q6, and they mentioned q9 being faster "unusual".

The behavior was observed deep into a golang program using (non-official) cgo bindings, so I've been trying to reproduce it with vanilla brotli, and I was finally able to!

---

### Exhibit

![brotli-q6-vs-q9](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7998310/34720298-7c503464-f53e-11e7-8c71-adaeb7fc273d.png)

This is not a "benchmark done right", but I've been playing with various input files for dozens of runs for the last 48h and I've noticed this pattern quite a bit.

### How I built brotli

I followed the instructions in `README.md` - this was run on windows-amd64, compiled with cmake:

```
$ mkdir out && cd out
$ cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=./installed ..
$ cmake --build . --config Release --target install
```

CMake auto-detected and used `Visual Studio 14 2015` to compile. Note that when I originally observed the performance difference, brotli's sources were built with gcc 7.2.0 (built by the MSYS2 project) via cgo.

The version of brotli I used was https://github.com/google/brotli/commit/da254cffdb160bd6ac1cd6ea01944099978cbb71 (HEAD of master at the time of this writing)

### The data

Here's a `none.br.zip` file, which is a zip archive (due to github issues limitations) containing a brotli-q9-compressed version of my sample input.

:package: [none.br.zip](https://github.com/google/brotli/files/1615018/none.br.zip)

The uncompressed input is a **2.2GB file**. It compresses particularly well (brotli performs splendidly in particular) because it's a patch file inspired by the original [BSDiff paper](http://www.daemonology.net/papers/bsdiff.pdf).

In particular, the file:

  * Starts with a 4-byte magic number
  * Is a stream of protobuf messages, prefixed by their length (dead-simple framing)
  * After a few header messages, most of the messages are bsdiff instructions (see the [protobuf schema](https://github.com/itchio/wharf/blob/master/bsdiff/bsdiff.proto))

It's supposed to compress well because bsdiff is basically:

  * Find slices from the "old" file and the "new" file that are similar
  * Compute byte-wise differences between these slices
  * The patch instruction is slice start in old and new, length, and the byte-wise difference (a large binary blob which, if everything goes well, should be *mostly* zero, and the differences should have nice repeating patterns, think `00000029000000000000290000000000`) - this is called `diff data`
  * If there's no slice similar enough, the new data is included as-is in the patch - this is called `extra data`

Other differences from the original bsdiff:

  * vanilla bsdiff uses bzip2 for its patch format
  * vanilla bsdiff patches are divided into three sections:
    * all instructions (without data)
    * all `diff data`
    * all `extra data`
  * ...whereas our version stores `diff data` and `extra data` directly after the relevant instruction - and limits an instruction's slice size. This lets us to streaming diff & apply with lower memory/disk requirements, with a slight compression penalty, I have a [tweet thread](https://twitter.com/fasterthanlime/status/790617515009437701) about this for the curious.

### Hypotheses

Although I've played with compression formats a lot, I cannot claim to understand its inner workings, but I've noticed the following:

  * In some parts of the code, LGBLOCK is adjusted differently for q>=9. I saw this [here](https://github.com/kothar/brotli-go/blob/771231d473d6c4896e97c2b6e3855f1afc0cdada/enc/encode.go#L23-L27) first, but I haven't checked that it doesn't appear somewhere else. Maybe q>=9 gets a smaller LGBLOCK, resulting in faster compression for this particular data ?
  * There's a `speed up RLE-ish data compression` commit (and this input data might be RLE-ish? depending on the meaning of it), but afaict I was seeing the performance difference before that commit.

### Conclusion

At this point I'm tempted to use q=9 rather than q=6-8 (our diff process is much slower than brotli compression anyway, and we're running it on a machine with many cores, so it's not a bottleneck), but I figured it'd be interesting to brotli developers and I might learn a thing or two in the process :)

issue_title: [Django] Requests from iOS time out when raven is enabled
issue_body:
I am having an issue with requests from iOS timing out when Raven is enabled. Any endpoints from my project will time out, but not endpoints that are included from third party apps. The issue disappears when excluding Raven from installed apps. This issue only occurs when sending a request from an iOS app. The same requests made using `httpie` or Postman are successful.

To make things more complicated, the request only times out on the iOS side. As soon as it times out, Django (or rather `runserver`) happily reports that the request was successful with a 200 status code.

I apologize for the lack of detailed information or a stack trace, but I have not been able to produce an actual error from the Django application. Please let me know if further information is needed or if you have ideas on things I should investigate further.

### URL Structure

As mentioned above, requests for URLs from third-party apps succeed, but any requests to URLs from my project fail.

#### Base URL File

```python
# project/project/urls.py

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^foo/', include('foo.urls'),    # Third party application works fine
    url(r'^my-app/', include('my_app.urls'),    # Application defined in my project fails
]
```

#### App URL File

```python
# project/my_app/urls.py

app_name = 'my-app'

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^bar/$', BarView.as_view(), name='bar-detail'),
]
```

### Views

The views from the project are mostly based on DRF's generic views (eg `generics.ListCreateView`). I don't believe the issue lies here, since some of the third-party apps used utilize the same generic views.

### Dependency Versions
* Django 2.0.3
* Django Rest Framework 3.7.7
* Raven 6.6.0

Downgrading to 6.1.0 causes the requests to work, but constantly logs errors about `URLConf`, which I believe is related to the Django 2 support that was 6.2.0 addresses.

issue_title: @base no longer prepended in local names
issue_body:
Hi,

I've noticed that the JSON-LD parser has changed its output w.r.t. prepending the @base URI to local names. The following code used to read [this file](https://gist.github.com/albertmeronyo/a2fff3db14362d539a1d99598476c92b#file-test-csv-metadata-json)

```
from rdflib import Graph

g = Graph()
g.parse('test.csv-metadata.json', format='json-ld')
```

and the resulting graph contained the triple 
```
N86eb3d54036d4bb98a416ec6022be35b http://www.w3.org/ns/csvw#aboutUrl https://iisg.amsterdam/resource/{_row}
```

However this has changed and now the triple is
```
N86eb3d54036d4bb98a416ec6022be35b http://www.w3.org/ns/csvw#aboutUrl {_row}
```

So it seems either this specific property is not expected to be a URI anymore (but a literal instead), or the parser is not prepending @base to URIs.

Could you help me out with this?

Thanks,
Albert

issue_title: SConstruct on darwin insists on Xcode and ignores command line tools
issue_body:
I am having trouble building Galsim on my Mac because I do not have Xcode installed but I am using the Apple command line tools. The problem is that an attempt is made to determine the Xcode version by invoking `xcodebuild` and this fails. Can the Xcode version determination be deferred until it might be needed? If TMV is not linking against BLAS then the whole test is irrelevant. The code is currently:
```python
p = subprocess.Popen(['xcodebuild','-version'], stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
xcode_version = p.stdout.readlines()[0].split()[1]
print 'XCode version is',xcode_version
if (platform.mac_ver()[0] >= '10.7' and #xcode_version < '5.1' and
    '-latlas' not in tmv_link and ('-lblas' in tmv_link or '-lcblas' in tmv_link)):
    print 'WARNING: The Apple BLAS library has been found not to be thread safe on'
    print '         Mac OS versions 10.7+, even across multiple processes (i.e. not'
    print '         just multiple threads in the same process.)  The symptom is that'
    print '         `scons tests` may hang when running nosetests using multiple'
    print '         processes.'
    if xcode_version < '5.1':
        print '         This seems to have been partially fixed with XCode 5.1, so we'
        print '         recommend upgrading to the latest XCode version.  However, even'
        print '         with 5.1, some systems still seem to have problems.'
        env['BAD_BLAS'] = True
    else:
        print '         This seems to have been partially fixed with XCode 5.1, so there'
        print '         is a good chance you will not have any problems.  But there are'
        print '         still occasional systems that fail when using multithreading with'
        print '         programs or modules that link to the BLAS library (such as GalSim).'
        print '         If you do have problems, the solution is to recompile TMV with'
        print '         the SCons option "WITH_BLAS=false".'
```
but changing it to something like:
```python
if (platform.mac_ver()[0] >= '10.7' and
    '-latlas' not in tmv_link and ('-lblas' in tmv_link or '-lcblas' in tmv_link)):
    p = subprocess.Popen(['xcodebuild','-version'], stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
    xcode_version = p.stdout.readlines()[0].split()[1]
    print 'XCode version is',xcode_version
   if xcode_version < '5.1':
```
would at least defer the breakage to a situation where BLAS is involved. I think the only real fix for this is to treat the clang version as a proxy for Xcode version.

- [fa-user-times]
- [fa-user-plus]
- etc.

Thanks, and amazing job on all the icons so far!

issue_title: New stacking size needed
issue_body: This is related to tickets: https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues/1181 https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues/5410 https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues/2000 Issue #5410 suggested a bad way to stack icons. While the icons will be the same size, they are placed in a larger container than if they were not stacked.. The current system of stacking places icons in a 2em container (`fa-stack`). This means the following two elements will be of different heights: ... This means users must either: • use larger icons for everything * use some shoddy css tricks This also means that the concept of having overlay icons is overcomplicated (#1181). Issue #2000 first brought this up. However it suggested fixing this -- and such a change would now be backwards compatible as many people have become accustomed to this. I would like to suggest a secondary stacking class be added, which maintains stacks of a consistent size..

issue_title: Failure to overwrite settings with python lib usage
issue_body:
I should be able to change the username, host, and password when executing a tower-cli command. I should still be able to do this when importing it in python and executing that same command.

That does not appear to be working. Consider the following, performing a list of users, giving a new value for `tower_host`.

```
>>> user_res.list(verbose=True, tower_host="http://localhost")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/alancoding/.virtualenvs/cli2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible_tower_cli-3.0.1-py2.7.egg/tower_cli/models/base.py", line 732, in list
    response = self.read(**kwargs)
  File "/Users/alancoding/.virtualenvs/cli2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible_tower_cli-3.0.1-py2.7.egg/tower_cli/models/base.py", line 516, in read
    r = client.get(url, params=kwargs)
  File "/Users/alancoding/.virtualenvs/cli2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 487, in get
    return self.request('GET', url, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/alancoding/.virtualenvs/cli2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible_tower_cli-3.0.1-py2.7.egg/tower_cli/api.py", line 137, in request
    'Right now it is: "%s".' % settings.host
tower_cli.utils.exceptions.ConnectionError: There was a network error of some kind trying to connect to Tower.

The most common  reason for this is a settings issue; is your "host" value in `tower-cli config` correct?
Right now it is: "http://0.0.0.0:8013".
```

It uses the _old_ value of the host. This is a component of the issue #149, but here, it is using the outright incorrect value.

We will probably be able to unit test this bug.

issue_title: [Question]How to handle a slave which has been disconnected from master for long time?
issue_body:
Let's say if I am using asynchronous streaming application with the below configuration in a 3 node cluster.

```
bootstrap:
  # this section will be written into Etcd:/<namespace>/<scope>/config after initializing new cluster
  # and all other cluster members will use it as a `global configuration`
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        archive_mode: "off"
        wal_level: hot_standby
        max_wal_senders: 10
        wal_keep_segments: 100
        max_replication_slots: 10
        hot_standby: "on"
        wal_log_hints: "on"
        unix_socket_directories: '/tmp'
        max_connections: 400
        shared_buffers: 250MB
        autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor: 0.05
        autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor: 0.10
        log_autovacuum_min_duration: 0
        autovacuum_naptime: 15s
        autovacuum_max_workers: 6
```

Let's say one of the slave node suddenly lose its connection to `master` for long time. 

1. In this case I think the size of XLOG on master will keep on building as the the XLOG are not being consumed by the disconnected slave's replication slot. So is there any setting in patorni configuration which will remove the slave and remove its replication slot if it is disconnected from master for x time duration? 
2. What is the recommended way to handle this case?

issue_title: Leader lock is not updated when restart is initiated
overriding json encoder
issue_body:
### Description
Overriding `json_encoder` is not propagated everywhere.
### Expected behaviour
connexion is using same dump encoder
### Actual behaviour
It uses flasks
### Steps to reproduce
We have a custom encoder for json to encode enum. 
So using 
```
      gender:
        type: string
        enum:
          - m
          - f
```
I got error `TypeError: <Gender.m: 'm'> is not JSON serializable`
According to docs, I overriden default encoder:
```python
application = connexion.App(__name__)
application.app.json_encoder = CustomJSONEncoder
```
but this introduces new problem:  
`On instance['stored_passengers'][0]['gender']:\n    None`
For some reason Flask json encoder dumps enum as `null`. This happens in `/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/apis/flask_api.py:_jsonify_data`.
`flask.json.dumps(data, indent=2)` returns `"gender": null`.  
`flask.json.dumps(data, indent=2, default=custom_encoder)` returns `"gender": "f"` as expected.
### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
Python 2.7.13
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
Version: 1.1.14

issue_title: Limiting fields returned in Result for ET_Subscriber
issue_body:
The documentation at https://code.exacttarget.com/apis-sdks/fuel-sdks/subscribers/subscriber-retrieve.html#pythonpost lists that we should be able to limit the number of fields returned.  It explicitly states the property 'ID' can be used.  If a call lists the props with only SubscriberKey, the results contain only the SubscriberKey.  As soon as I add 'ID' to the list of props, the results return all fields. 

Example:
    debug = False
    stubObj = ET_Client.ET_Client(False, debug)
    getSubscriber = ET_Client.ET_Subscriber()
    getSubscriber.auth_stub = stubObj
    getSubscriber.props = ['ID','SubscriberKey','EmailAddress']

This will return all fields.  Where as the following only returns the explicitly requested fields:
    debug = False
    stubObj = ET_Client.ET_Client(False, debug)
    getSubscriber = ET_Client.ET_Subscriber()
    getSubscriber.auth_stub = stubObj
    getSubscriber.props = ['SubscriberKey','EmailAddress']

I'm not sure if this is an issue with API itself, the documentation being incorrect, or the SDK.  If I can provide more info, please advise.  I'm fairly new to Python, so please go easy on me.

issue_title: [gtk3] Right click not passed to apps
issue_body:
Start an application that is interested in right-click events, such as Midnight Commander. Click with the right button.

Expected: Event sent to the app.

Actual: The menu pops up.

This was indeed not possible with gtk2-based vte. You need to first send the right-click event to VTE, and then depending on whether VTE handled it or not, open up (or not) the menu. You might want to check the modifier keys, e.g. always open the menu on Shift + right-click.

See the [gnome-terminal patch here](https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=13299) for what to do exactly.

issue_title: Cookie based authentication in addition to current available ones
issue_body:
Implement cookie based authentication as described in https://developer.atlassian.com/jiradev/jira-apis/jira-rest-apis/jira-rest-api-tutorials/jira-rest-api-example-cookie-based-authentication

The idea is to take the user credentials (username and password), authenticate using the credentials in the body of a POST only, as described in the link above, and then let the cookies in subsequent requests be used for authorization.

Further, upon cookie expiration, re-authentication should work transparently.

As a side note, we've been requested by Atlassian to use this method instead of Basic Authentication due to heave additional resource utilization when using Basic Authentication, because each request requires the authorization to be processed. Cookie based authorization is thus better from a performance point of view (as perceived by the server).

Also, please note this is different from the use case described in #72 "Support login by cookies. Don't need to take username password every time" or #324 "Cookies based authentication support", because our use case doesn't have to do with persisting the cookie. We still want to accept username and password and have everything working as similar as before as possible.

Finally, the idea of removing authorization from every request, as described in https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/issues/324#issuecomment-292647132, would probably achieve similar performance improvements, but wouldn't lead to automatic re-authentication when the cookie expires, as far as I can tell.

I've implemented this authentication and will follow with a PR shortly.

issue_title: Hardcoding of file path of "sd-messages.h" preventing using this in build systems
issue_body:
Hi,

We are trying to build python-systemd for a different architecture using yocto build system, but due to the hardcoding of the file path of ```sd-messages.h``` in ```setup.py``` we are unable to do so unless the systemd header files are installed on the build system and are of the same version as that of the target build

Offending lines:
~~~~
constants = [line.split()[1]
                         for line in open("/usr/include/systemd/sd-messages.h")
                         if line.startswith('#define SD_MESSAGE_')]
~~~~

The code should instead take a relative path to systemd headers and use that instead of the absolute path. Will send you out a pull request for the same if it is acceptable or please suggest an alternative.

issue_title: Travis CI is slow, move to container based infrastructure on Travis
issue_body: Travis CI is becomes quite slow to start, moving to [Travis CI's container-based infrastructure](http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/workers/container-based-infrastructure/) should speed things up and may allow for caching of dependencies.  The difficulty is removing sudo commands, which are currently limited to apt-get.  The [apt addon](http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/apt/) can be used to solve this for gfortran but libglpk-dev is not white-listed and will break if/when Travis moves to a more recent version of Ubunutu with a version of GLPK which is not supported by pyglpk (4.53+).  Building a glpk and new pygkpk conda packages should be possible and would solve this block in addition to allowing easier installs of older and pyglpk compatible version of this library on modern Linux systems.

issue_title: Problem with NTLM-authenticate proxy for HTTPS CONNECT
issue_body:
Hi,
I try to use HttpNtlmAuth to use for a NTLM proxy authentication request.

tested with Python 2.7.10 (default, May 23 2015, 09:40:32) [MSC v.1500 32 bit (Intel)]:

```
import requests
from requests_ntlm import HttpNtlmAuth
from requests.packages.urllib3.util.retry import Retry
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter

user = 'WORKGROUP\user' # or '.\user'
password = '1234'

http_proxy  = 'http://127.0.0.1:8080'
proxy_dict = {'http': http_proxy, 'https': http_proxy}

retries = Retry(total=10,
                read=5,
                connect=6,
                #these options don't seem to help
                #backoff_factor=1,
                #method_whitelist=(['HEAD', 'TRACE', 'GET', 'POST', 'CONNECT', 'OPTIONS', 'DELETE']), 
                #status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504, 403, 407]
                )

session = requests.Session()
session.verify = False
session.mount('http://', HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retries))
session.mount('https://', HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retries))

session.proxies = proxy_dict
session.auth = HttpNtlmAuth(user, password)

#this works: HTTP GET
r = session.get('http://neverssl.com/')
print(r.text)

#HTTPS CONNECT fails!
r2 = session.get('https://www.google.com')
print(r2.text)

```
The last error message from the call stack is:
```
`ConnectionError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='www.google.com', port=443): 
Max retries exceeded with url: /
 (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', 
error('Tunnel connection failed: 407 Proxy Authentication Required',)))`

```

I checked with Wireshark (and Npcap for loopback connection monitoring) and the HTTP connection looks like this:
HTTP GET:  
Packet 1, request:   GET http://neverssl.com/
Packet 2, response: HTTP/1.1 407 Proxy Authentication Required
Packet 3, request:    GET http://neverssl.com/ HTTP/1.1 , Proxy-authorization: NTLM TlRMTV...
Packet 4, response: HTTP/1.1 407 Proxy Authentication Required, Proxy-Authenticate: NTLM TlRMT....
Packet 5, request:   GET http://neverssl.com/ HTTP/1.1 Proxy-authorization: NTLM TlRMTVNT...(much longer than last time)
Packet 6, request: HTTP/1.1 200 OK


HTTPS CONNECT:
Packet 1, request:   CONNECT www.google.com:443 HTTP/1.0  (the whole request is literally just this one line)
Packet 2, response: HTTP/1.1 407 Proxy Authentication Required,  Proxy-Authenticate: NTLM
-> Error ConnectionError

Ok, how can this be fixed ?
Is this a bug in HttpNtlmAuth that does not set the number of retries ?

issue_body:
`gcloud.iterator.Iterator` is a base class, relying on a `path` for the API method.  I think it would be clearer if it wrapped around a method of a client (or other domain object), where that method takes `page_token`, `page_size`, etc., along with other named arguments in `**kw`.  We could then ditch all the derived classes, and just use the one, common implementation.  E.g.:

```python
from gcloud.iterator import Iterator
from gcloud.pubsub import Client

client = Client()
topics = list(Iterator(client.list_topics))
subs = list(Iterator(client.list_subscriptions, topic_name='my-topic'))
```

issue_title: Impossible to set non-default storage project since 0.5.0
issue_body:
The upgrade to 0.5.0 broke the public API without notes in the Release by removing `storage.get_connection(project='foo')` function.

Moreover, it seems that the `Connection` class no longer has a `project` parameter. Neither `storage.get_bucket` nor `storage.lookup_bucket` take `project` as a parameter. Curiously, you can override it in `create_bucket` and `list_buckets`.

The only way to set a project for these is through `_helpers.set_default_project`, which makes it incredibly hard to operate on buckets in multiple projects, since you need to set the default all the time, exposing yourself to race conditions in case of multithreading.

Can we put back the `project` into `Connection`? AFAIK the `Connection`'s credentials only make sense for a given `project` anyway.

issue_title: callbacks should be namespace aware
issue_body: get_callback_url() contains only the pipeline ID but no information about the current namespace.  The default _CallbackHandler will do the pipeline lookup in the default namespace, so if the original pipeline was running within a non-default namespace, the callback handler wont be able to find it

issue_labels: [{'id': 544604260, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20error-reporting', 'name': 'api: error-reporting', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2+', 'name': 'priority: p2+', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This is a tracking work item to get the Error Reporting GAPIC into this library:

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gapic-google-cloud-error-reporting-v1beta1

@steren and I agree that the existing (small) `report` and `report_exception` is what we want to keep, along with possible more framework integrations in the future. So I think what we primarily want is the same thing as with done with Logging where we add the GAPIC as a dependency, and then fallback to the existing code if gRPC is disabled.

One small question is if and to what extent we want to expose the rest of the GAPIC besides events.report via this library. There are a few API calls that will likely only be used by power users, but it might be worth having some sort of pass-through mechanism. Unless I'm mistake, it looks like so far we either wrap all the GAPIC calls or pass-through completely, but we haven't done both, so not sure exactly how this would look.

cc @bjwatson @jgeewax @omaray in case you have comments.

issue_title: [Vision]  add a new function 'detect_all' in image.py
issue_body:
There is a function `detect` in image.py for detecting multiple feature types. Could we add a function called `detect_all` to detect all feature types at one time? This is my proposal:

```python
    def detect_all(self, limit=10):
        """Detect multiple feature types.

        :type limit: int
        :param limit: The number of faces to try and detect.

        :rtype: list
        :returns: List of
                  :class:`~google.cloud.vision.entity.EntityAnnotation`.
        """

        features = [Feature(x, limit) for x in FeatureTypes.__dict__.keys() if x[:1] != '_']
        annotations = self.detect(features)
        return annotations[0]
```

issue_body:
Load jobs have attributes to specify partitioning of the table as mentioned in the below link - 
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs

It doesnt look like the current bigquery python client supports the "configuration.load.timePartitioning.field" attributes of neigther load job nor a table. These are important attributes to configure a load job. Let me know if these are already implemented and I missed them, but after browsing the code, I dont think they are implemented. If they are not implemented, I believe this is an important feature for this api to be useful.

**Edit by @tswast, to better track sub-tasks for this issue**

This feature adds the ability to partition a table by a timestamp or datetime column. Changes required:

- [ ] New `google.cloud.bigquery.[table].TimePartitioning` class to describe time partitioning definition.
  - [ ] `TimePartitioning.type_` property (defaults to 'DAY' in TimePartitioning constructor)
  - [ ] `TimePartitionion.field` property (string)
  - [ ] `TimePartitioning.expiration_ms` (int, but stored in API representation as a string)
  - [ ] `TimePartitioning.require_partition_filter` property (bool)
  - [ ] `TimePartitioning.to_api_repr()`
  - [ ] `TimePartitioning.from_api_repr()`
- [ ] New `QueryJobConfig.time_partitioning` property on query configuration to set time partitioning for destination tables.
- [ ] New `LoadJobConfig.time_partitioning` property on load job configuration to set time partitioning for destination tables.
- [ ] New `Table.time_partitioning` property to describe time partitioning definition.
- [ ] New `TableListItem.time_partitioning` property to describe time partitioning definition.

issue_title: pubsub: flow control creates a lot of RuntimeErrors
issue_body:
When the `thread` policy gets a callback request for `"lease"`, `BasePolicy.lease` is called *from the request consumer thread*.

If this lease call determines that there are too many messages currently being handled, it tries to stop the consumer thread. But this is done by calling `helper_threads.stop('callback requests worker')` which in turns tries to join the "callback requests worker" thread (after having added a STOP item to its queue).
This raises a `RuntimeError: cannot join current thread`, which is caught by the QueueCallbackThread and logged. I don't think the error in itself doesn't cause any harm because the subscription is then re-opened once the load is low enough and the helper threads gets restarted. 

It's still ugly though because the end of the `helper_thread.stop(…)` call is never executed and the `self._helper_threads` dict stil contains the dead thread.

issue_title: measure "density" of points space for archive
issue_body: This is a bit abstract... but for some given space, one would want to discover where "lots" of points are, and other times where points are "missing".  Both are useful.  For example, within a given N-dimensional box, one might sample the regions of space to get an idea of how a function performs in each region… one would want to find where there are "holes" in the database, and then sample there next as opposed to randomly selecting a region.

issue_title: Kill off PEX_PYTHON
issue_body:
The pex runtime currently supports a configuration for a PEX_PYTHON variable in a pexrc file. This variable allows a user to define a Python Interpreter to override the default interpreter when executing the pex. 

This has been replaced by a PEX_PYTHON_PATH variable, that allows the user to specify a colon-separated list of paths to Python interpreters with the aim of being able support interpreter selection against a list of interpreter override candidates.

The goal of this issue is to completely kill off PEX_PYTHON support in the pex codebase in favor of the new PEX_PYTHON_PATH pexrc variable.

issue_title: Tasks that call safe_mkdir(results_dir, clean=True) silently overwrite symlink with real directory
issue_body:
This will later cause the Exceptions when the cache_manager attempts to unlink the `vt.results_dir`,  something like:
```
 Exception message: [Errno 1] Operation not permitted: '/Users/yic/workspace/source/.pants.d/resolve/node/252d64521cf9/<target.id>/current'.
```
This situation is somewhat rare - it can only happen when an invalid VT unexpectedly has contents inside the results_dir, perhaps from a failed resolve or an error during task execution.

Additionally, not only is the symlink silently replaced, but the actual clean is never executed - the clean will remove the symlink, but the unique current_results_dir is not dereferenced. So not only is the results_dir symlink bugged but the bad or failed state is preserved within the canonical path.

This is a limitation of safe_mkdir, in that it has no concept of the unique properties of symlinks. My instinct is that the the clean operation should be removed since the clean does not work on symlinks like the `results_dir`, which is the most likely directory for a task to interact with.

For now, a workaround within the cache_manager is to simply make sure that the `current_results_dir` that the symlink points to is always empty when passed to tasks. This assumes that the clean=True calls are always trying to clear old state, as opposed to happening mid-way through execution. This assumption is true for the call sites I am aware of, both inside our repo and pants itself.

This causes the clean operation to noop and therefore preserve the symlink. It also catches the additional failure state of the failed clean, since we have now forced it within the cache manager.

The intermediate workaround is going out soon, this issue merely tracks it.

issue_title: jvm search order is surprising
issue_body:
From `distribution.py`:

```
    First looks through the paths listed for this operating system in the --jvm-distributions-paths
    map. Then looks in JDK_HOME and JAVA_HOME if defined, falling back to a search on the PATH.
```

I think this search order is backwards.  `PATH` and `JAVA_HOME` are explicit configurations given by the operator.    All else being equal (ie it still satisfies min/max version requirements) I'd expect pants to use the version of java I told my OS was the default.

issue_title: Request: Support Single Unzipped Source for AWS::Serverless::Function
issue_body:
In the simplest case, a serverless Lambda function may be a single source code file with no external dependencies. eg (yeah, Python):

```
import os
import boto3

import logging
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

def handler(event, context):
    logging.info("Hello World")
```

Under these conditions it would be nice for the SAM Template to let me point to this unzipped source file.
Currently, [the template's CodeUri](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/examples/2016-10-31/hello_world/template.yaml) expects me to point to a zip archive, requiring me to add a step to our build pipeline to take the source and zip it up.

It would be nice for either CodeUri to recognize when it is pointed to a non-zip file, or have the ability of using a different property (eg: SimpleSource) for these cases, and have SAM or awscli or CloudFormation zip that file up for me (it seems CloudFormation expects it to be zipped by the time the change set is executed)

issue_title: Document.add_paragraph performance drops with document length
issue_body:
Recently, I used python-docx to combine several hundred blog posts into a single Word document for editing. While doing this, I was surprised to note that with every paragraph added using `Document.add_paragraph`, the time required to add the next paragraph increased.

Fortunately, python-docx offers an alternate method of adding paragraphs: `Paragraph.insert_paragraph_before`. I used this to compare performance, and indeed, the difference is huge. I suspect that somewhere in the code for `add_paragraph`, every single paragraph is being needlessly iterated over—this has the whiff of [Shlemiel the painter’s algorithm](https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2001/12/11/back-to-basics/) at work.

Here's a test program I wrote and the resulting test numbers:

```python
from docx import Document
from timeit import default_timer as timer

def p_add(doc, leader):
    doc.add_paragraph()

def p_insert(doc, leader):
    leader.insert_paragraph_before()

def time_func(func, iterations, steps):
    doc = Document()
    leader = doc.add_paragraph()

    for i in range(steps):
        start = timer()
        for i in range(round(iterations / steps)):
            func(doc, leader)
        yield timer() - start

def print_times(func, iterations, steps):
    total = 0
    for step, time in enumerate(time_func(func, iterations, steps)):
        total += time
        print('  {0}: {1}'.format(step, time))
    print('  ---------------------')
    print(' ', total)

# -----------------------------------------

NUM_PARAGRAPHS = 50000
STEPS = 10

print('Paragraph count:', NUM_PARAGRAPHS)
print('Step count:', STEPS)

print('\nAdd method:')
print_times(p_add, NUM_PARAGRAPHS, STEPS)

print('\nInsert method:')
print_times(p_insert, NUM_PARAGRAPHS, STEPS)
```

Output:

```
Paragraph count: 50000
Step count: 10

Add method:

  1: 0.6364703527799802
  2: 0.9327317248817353
```

issue_title: No Format in NameIDPolicy causes use of transient always with SAML frontend
issue_body: It is acceptable SAML for an <AuthnRequest> to include a <NameIDPolicy> element that has the AllowCreate attribute but not the Format attribute. When the SAML frontend receives such a request it should consult the SP metadata to determine which NameID format to use. Instead if the Format attribute is not present the request format in the SP metadata is ignored and transient is always used.

issue_title: namespace API URLs by app, with an API root
issue_body:
Proposal by @jamalex migrated from [Trello card](https://trello.com/c/iQ2nF7K9/89-api-urls-are-namespaced-by-app-with-an-api-root):

Have a top-level `/api/` in `urls.py` that imports a base `api_urls.py`. issue_title: escape_gt_in_attrs
issue_body: Why is there no `escape_gt_in_attrs` counterpart of `escape_lt_in_attrs`? Optimistic regex tag-soup readers do negative lookaheads for `>` to determine the closing point of a tag. They’re just as important to escape in atts as `<` for compatibility with everything.

issue_title: Local file not working with replay
issue_body:
With a local file `mkdc.cdxj` in the directory of executing after installing via pip:

```sh
$ ipwb replay mkdc.cdxj
Executing first-run procedure on provided sample data.
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
Retrieving URI-Ms from mkdc.cdxj
File mkdc.cdxj does not exist locally, fetching remote
No scheme in path, assuming IPFS hash and fetching...
HTTPError: 500 Server Error: Internal Server Error for url: http://127.0.0.1:5001/api/v0/cat?stream-channels=true&encoding=json&arg=mkdc.cdxj
```

IPWB replay is treating the filename as an IPFS hash.

This was likely introduced when specifying an IPFS hash was implemented. All scenarios require more automatic testing.

issue_title: Cannot clear trackingUrlTemplate from Customer without supplying customerId
issue_body:
The issue is that the library appears to be deleting the customer entity before generating the outbound soap request as it contains only an empty value for trackingUrlTemplate. The workaround is to also supply the customerId in the payload so the customer entity isn't removed. The customerId (or any other value) should not be necessary for this operation to succeed. This may be related to the issue #62. It was occurring on the latest client library code on Python 2.7 as of 3 days ago.

```
  try:
    customer = customer_service.mutate({u'trackingUrlTemplate': u'', 'xsi_type': 'Customer'})
    #customer = customer_service.mutate({u'trackingUrlTemplate': u'', 'xsi_type': 'Customer', 'customerId': 7894463870}) # this line will work
```

issue_title: interfaces.yaml ignore option doesn't have any effect with charm build
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

`charm tools version: charm-tools==2.1.2`

I am using: `Xenial`

## Issue/Feature

### I expect/expected the following

When building a charm, it pulls in interfaces and layers as needed.  There is an optional 'ignore' option that can go in the layer.yaml or interface.yaml to add specs to the DEFAULT_IGNORE list such that it won't copy the ignored files/paths into the built charm.

Unfortunately, it doesn't work with interfaces as the ignore option in interface.yaml is not used.  It does work with layer.yaml files.

This means that we can't ignore (for example) interface specific unit tests that we don't wish to carry over to the final charm.

How to reproduce: create an interface, with some files that you don't wish included.  Add ignore option in interface.yaml.

### What I got

On build the files shouldn't be copied.  The files are copied into the resultant charm.

## Additional info:

Chatting on IRC with cory_fu and bcsaller the possible culprit is at: https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/tactics.py#L159 in that it doesn't use `self.interface.config.ignores` as part of the list of ignores.
