issue_title: Replace functions from raiden.utils with eth-utils equivalents
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

We currently define some helper functions ourselves in the `raiden.utils` module. We should use the ones that are available in `eth-utils` instead.

### ToDo

Any issue can be solved independently

- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.decode_hex()` with `eth_utils.decode_hex()` (Note that the current `decode_hex` handles more input types, so this might require more changes)
- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.encode_hex()` with `eth_utils.encode_hex()` (Note that the current `encode_hex` handles more input types, so this might require more changes) (#1580)
- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.isaddress()` with `eth_utils.is_binary_address()`. (#1513)
- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.address_decoder()` with `eth_utils.to_canonical_address()`.
- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.address_encoder()` with `eth_utils.to_normalized_address()`. (#1527)
- [x] Replace `raiden.utils.quantity_decoder()` with `eth_utils.to_int()`. 
- [ ] Replace `[un]hexlify` with `eth_utils.(en|de)code_hex` wherever possible and ensure hex-encoded strings are kept `0x`-prefixed: See #1654

issue_title: channel_manager.getChannelParticipants() returns an empty list
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

As can be seen from [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/888#issuecomment-322793739) comment the channel manager's [`getChannelParticipants()`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/ChannelManagerContract.sol#L37) returns an empty list even if there are currently open channels.

We have experienced this in the testnet with a channel manager for the "Raiden Test Token".

It can be verified by the following for a token on ropsten:

```js
var channel_manager_address = "0xff88b0bc757631a3de768bcb89d2c0664fc8b88d";
```

issue_title: Update API documentation for Red Eyes
issue_body:
## Problem Definition
With the new TokenNetwork design there will be changes to the [Raiden API](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/api/rest.py). We need to make sure this is kept up to date. This is ideally done, when the code is updated. If it is, then this issue should check that it's correct and if the documentation has not been updated according to the code, this issue must take care of updating the documentation according to the code changes.

This issue is an ongoing issue that is dependent on changes to the API, and should not be considered done until the documentation and code is in sync prior to a release.

The current documentation is available [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/docs/rest_api.rst)

## Tasks
- [ ] Check if the documentation is up to date according to code changes

If docs are not up-to-date:
- [ ] Update documentation according to code changes

issue_title: Order of %preview and %run
issue_body:
`%preview` works in

```
%preview -n abc.txt -c ~/docker.yml -r docker
%run -r docker -c ~/docker.yml
sh:
   echo abc > abc.txt
```
but not in 

```
%run -r docker -c ~/docker.yml
%preview -n abc.txt -c ~/docker.yml -r docker
sh:
   echo abc > abc.txt
```

because `%run` execute the rest of the cell as sos script... We should at least warn users about this.

issue_title: Use constant time compare in state validation
[tune] Implement Async SHA
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

Hyperband has a pretty big overhead for small tasks. A followup work, https://openreview.net/forum?id=S1Y7OOlRZ, provides an alternative by formulating "buckets" as "rungs" like a ladder. The algorithm seems reasonably straightforward to implement; much simpler than HyperBand. We can use this to compare against median stopping rule and hyperband.

issue_title: [xray] Make it so actors are started in parallel
issue_body:
At the moment, in xray actors are started in sequence. Starting a new Python interpreter takes a few hundred ms, so creating 1000 actors for example is very slow. This can be reproduced with

```
ray start --head --use-raylet
```

```
import ray

ray.init(redis_address="<ip_address>:<port>", use_raylet=True)
@ray.remote
class Actor(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass
```

```
As = [Actor.remote() for i in range(10000)]
```

issue_title: More warnings
issue_body:
I think the following flags should be added to the defaults:

```
-fsanitize=address
```

see https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AddressSanitizer.html

and also
```
-Wshadow
```

seem useful flags for writing clean code. The first one can help identify illegal access mistakes and the second one can help finding shadowing bugs like in e.g. nested loops where the loop variables are shadowed in one of the inner loops.

issue_title: Add a Part.move() function
issue_body:
The purpose of this function would be to copy-paste or cut-paste the part instance/part model from under a parent and moving it to another parent, with (optionally) all the values of its properties and the sub-tree. 

Based on the [ICT-549](https://atlas.ke-works.net/jira/browse/ICT-549) ticket filed by Stefan. 

However, I am not sure we should implement this in Pykechain or just have it as a separate script (in which of course, we are using Pykechain). What do you think, @jberends ?

issue_title: About LPversion and LPhelp
issue_body:
`from LightPipes import *` isn't good practice in python, although it seems nicer in this project, I prefer
`import LightPipes as lp` because it follows the convention of most python libraries.

The biggest problem of `import *` is it will pollute global namespace, we can add [\_\_all\_\_](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/modules.html#importing-from-a-package) to
`__init__.py`  to avoid this(LPversion and LPhelp look ugly).

Do you prefer convenience or comprehensive?

issue_title: Add a blocked task watchdog
issue_body:
In [this stackoverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51680089/trio-lib-queue-get-and-put), someone gets very confused because they're using `time.sleep` and never using `await`, so one of their tasks seems to run correctly but the other doesn't. There's no indication of what's going on. Easy to fix once you know how, but if you don't, then how are you supposed to figure it out?

We could, pretty cheaply, add a watchdog to catch such situations. What I'm imagining is that when entering `trio.run`, we'd spawn a system task that spins up a thread and watches for the main loop to become stuck. In the main run loop, we'd send it messages occasionally, whenever we (a) start processing a batch of tasks, (b) stop processing a bunch of tasks. If the watchdog thread detects that the time between (a) and (b) exceeds, say, 5 seconds, then it should print an informative message and use the [`faulthandler`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/faulthandler.html) module to dump everyone's stack traces. I think this might be cheap enough it could be enabled-by-default.

issue_title: ContextTracer.finish() does not end remaining spans
issue_body:
From reading the documentation of Tracer.finish() it looks like calling finish() on a tracer object should end all remaining spans and send them to exporters. However, it looks like the current implementation 
just [resets the internal span list](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/tracers/context_tracer.py#L54). This usually isn't a problem unless you get into a state where all spans are not ended properly.

I am proposing that we update ContextTracer.finish() to end all spans in ContextTracer._spans_list before resetting state. 

Happy to submit a PR for this change if everyone agrees on the proposal.

issue_title: Problems with multiprocessing caused by the zipfile module
issue_body:
I was investigating on a really strange issue today: I was using `fmpy` in our project together with a larger multiprocessing setup (doing parallel simulations) and I encountered a strange error (which was hard to debug due to multiprocess stuff) when running on Linux (funnily, Windows worked). The result was a `BrokenProcessPool` error or a `queue.Full` error. 
After several hours of slicing away code line by code line, I ended up with the following script:

```python
def use_fmpy(*args):
    import fmpy
    return fmpy.read_model_description('bouncingBall_me.fmu') 

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import tables  ##### TRIGGER HERE

    import concurrent.futures as cf
    with cf.ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as pool:
        futures = []
        for i in range(10):
            future = pool.submit(use_fmpy, None)
            futures.append(future)
        cf.wait(futures)
```

What made it even harder was the fact that the `import tables` line triggered the error in the multiprocessing subprocesses. With the pytables import I got the error, without pytables, the error wasn't appearing.

So I first suspected pytables for being the source of the problem, but after a while I took a closer look at the `read_model_description` function. (BTW I tried out all available fmpy versions) The first lines of this function look like this:

https://github.com/CATIA-Systems/FMPy/blob/35245dd8bf2b8be2a8e750b04bd29df4557d3032/fmpy/model_description.py#L210-L212

It uses the `zipfile` module to read the `modelDescription.xml` file from the FMU file. Putting a `return` just before this code block made my example above work in all cases, so it must have had to do with the `zipfile` use.

Googling brought immediately a possible issue: [It seems that the `zipfile` module is *not threadsafe*](https://stackoverflow.com/a/22853969/195476). So my idea was to replace the `zipfile` use by a call to `unzip`:

```python
import subprocess
root = etree.fromstring(subprocess.check_output(
    ['unzip', '-p', filename, 'modelDescription.xml'])) 
```

And surprise: it worked! No more problems with `BrokenProcessPool` or `queue.Full` errors.

So my question here: would it be possible that you replace all the `zipfile` usages by something more robust? I would really love to use `fmpy` instead of `pyfmi`, but this issue cost me already 5 hours of debugging and it is currently a blocker for us.

issue_title: Add hard size restrictions to initializers and inputs
issue_body:
Currently, inputs and initializers can be overridden, potentially with values of different shapes or sizes. Depending on the graph, this may or may not work at all, and in the meantime being unable to rely on static sizes complicates certain operations on the graph (for example, converting to a backend which requires certain operators to specify their size at graph construction time rather than at runtime).

I propose to add to the model explicit sizes for inputs, which may optionally be inferred (but not modified or overridden) from initializers.

issue_title: DQN always uses SGD as optimizer?
issue_body:
Hey everybody!

I just noticed something odd in the DQN code. When compiling the model, you can specify an optimizer and give it to the compile function like this:

https://github.com/keras-rl/keras-rl/blob/f19b75de06f1c7f902a0cbdaec7203b355d81765/examples/dqn_atari.py#L101

But taking a look at the code inside "compile", the "custom" optimizer is not used at all but it is overwritten with the SGD:
https://github.com/keras-rl/keras-rl/blob/f19b75de06f1c7f902a0cbdaec7203b355d81765/rl/agents/dqn.py#L173

Is this a mistake or did I get something wrong?

issue_title: Sync readme with implementation
Use AoikLiveReload or similar for live reloading
issue_body:
https://github.com/AoiKuiyuyou/AoikLiveReload/blob/master/src/aoiklivereload/aoiklivereload.py

We only do reloading on per-module (file) granularity. That is not enough because any other code will not be reloaded.

The actual count on Twitter is 741 liked posts. However this command only outputs 661 tweets. It is missing 80 favorites -- how come? A hunch is that the --favorites flag does not return the tweets that the user tweeted and favorited (i.e. if Liam favorited his own tweet)

### Environment Details
MacOS and Linux terminals

issue_title: LINGER Not Working for Device
issue_body:
With this simple STREAMER device, sometimes it will print that it receives all 1000 messages, sometimes it will be ~300, sometimes more or less -- it's so intermittent as to how many messages it receives.

https://gist.github.com/1302163

However, if you uncomment the time.sleep(5) line at the end of the client(), it keeps the client open long enough for the socket to finish sending the messages to the worker and prints that it received all 1000 messages.

This looks like LINGER may not be working in the device.

It usually runs fine the first time, but subsequent times it still comes up short:

$ python device_test.py
...
0 got message! #734
0 got message! #735
0 got message! #736
0 got message! #737
0 got message! #738

I have tested this with pyzmq 2.1.9 and the latest GitHub version on both Fedora and Ubuntu with ZMQ 2.1.4 and 2.1.11.
